title: Connecting a Client to a Server
concept: Establishing the initial handshake and capability negotiation between client and server.

exercises.prompt: To recap our prior concepts: What is the main role of a client in the Model Context Protocol (MCP) architecture?

options.text: It acts as the host that initiates connections to servers to access tools and data.
It acts as the primary database storing raw training data for the LLM.
It is the physical hardware that runs the GPU clusters.

exercises.explain: An MCP client acts as the host (like an editor or IDE) that connects to and coordinates various tools and data sources provided by MCP servers.

cards.title: The Magic Behind the Connection
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how an AI coding assistant instantly knows what tools are available on your local computer? It all starts with a silent, ultra-fast digital handshake when the client connects to the server.

cards.title: Capability Negotiation
cards.body: In the Model Context Protocol (MCP), a client connects to a server by sending an 'initialize' request. This starts a negotiation where both sides declare their protocol version and what features (like tools or prompts) they support.

cards.title: The Diplomatic Handshake
cards.body: Think of the handshake like two foreign diplomats meeting at a summit. Before discussing heavy matters, they greet each other, state what languages they speak, and list the protocols they support to ensure they can work together smoothly.

cards.title: A Live Handshake
cards.body: When a Python-based MCP client starts up, it launches a server sub-process and sends a JSON-RPC message declaring its client info. The server replies with its own details, confirming it can share its 'file-search' tool.

cards.title: Who is in Control?
cards.body: You might think the client decides everything the server can do. In reality, the client must ask the server what capabilities it supports during the handshake, preventing errors before any tools are actually called.

cards.title: Connection vs. Negotiation
cards.body: Compare how a simple client connection differs from a full MCP handshake.

left.text: Plugs in and assumes both sides speak the exact same language.

right.text: Exchanges protocol versions and lists allowed tools before executing code.

exercises.prompt: What is the very first message an MCP client sends to a server to start the connection?

options.text: An 'initialize' request containing client info and supported protocol versions
A direct command to execute a specific tool on the host machine
A prompt asking the LLM to write code for the connection
An authentication token to lock down the server immediately

exercises.explain: The 'initialize' request is the standard first step in the MCP protocol where the client introduces itself and asks for the server's capabilities.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The capability negotiation process allows both the client and server to declare what features they support.
exercises.explain: Both the client and the server must explicitly state their capabilities during the handshake so they know how to interact.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of the initial connection phase:
exercises.sentence: Before exchanging any tools, the client and server must complete a handshake to agree on capabilities.
exercises.explain: The client and server perform a handshake to negotiate versions and capabilities before exchanging data.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following represents an actual AI-generated MCP client initialization request payload?

options.text: fetch('/api/connect', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ connect: true }) })
SELECT * FROM connections WHERE status = 'handshake_pending' LIMIT 1;

exercises.explain: Option A shows a typical, clean JSON-RPC initialization request used in MCP, declaring client details and protocol version, while the others are unrelated web development or database snippets.

exercises.prompt: You are building a custom MCP client from scratch. What is the correct sequence of events to establish a fully functional connection with a server?

options.text: Start sending tool execution requests immediately without waiting for the handshake response.
Hardcode the server's tools inside the client code before establishing a transport stream.
Launch the server process, send the 'initialize' request, verify capabilities, and send an 'initialized' notification.
Skip the initialization phase entirely and jump straight to the notification protocol.

exercises.explain: Establishing a connection requires first launching the server process (standard I/O), initiating the handshake via 'initialize', verifying the negotiated capabilities, and then telling the server it is ready to receive requests.
